sections.title: Understanding the German Akkusativ
sections.description: In German, the Accusative case is primarily used for the Direct Object of a sentence. This is the person or thing that is directly affected by the action of the verb. If the Nominative case is the "doer" (subject), the Accusative is the "receiver."

sections.title: 1. Identifying the Accusative: The "Who" or "What"
sections.description: To find the accusative object, ask yourself: "Who or what is being [verbed]?"

example.note: What am I buying? → Den Apfel (The apple). Therefore, "apple" is in the accusative case.

sections.title: 2. Articles and Adjective Endings
sections.description: The most important thing to remember about the Accusative is that only the masculine gender changes. Feminine, Neuter, and Plural articles remain the same as they are in the Nominative case.

content.adjective_note: If you use words like dieser (this), jener (that), or welcher (which), the adjective following them follows the same pattern as the definite article (-en for masculine/plural, -e for feminine/neuter).

sections.title: 3. Personal Pronouns
sections.description: When a person is the direct object (e.g., "He sees me"), we must use the Accusative form of the pronoun.

example.note: Here, "you" is the person being loved, so du becomes dich.

sections.title: 4. When to Use the Accusative
sections.description: There are three main triggers for the Accusative case:

triggers.title: A. Accusative Verbs
triggers.description: Most German verbs require the accusative because they involve doing something to something else.

subcategories.label: Physical Actions
subcategories.verbs: essen (to eat), trinken (to drink), kaufen (to buy)
subcategories.translation: He eats a cake — Masculine change.

subcategories.label: Mental/Sensory
subcategories.verbs: hören (to hear), verstehen (to understand), suchen (to look for)
subcategories.translation: We are looking for the dog.

triggers.title: B. Accusative Prepositions
triggers.description: Certain prepositions are always followed by the accusative, regardless of the verb:

triggers.title: C. Two-Way Prepositions (Movement vs. Location)
triggers.description: Some prepositions (an, auf, hinter, in, neben, über, unter, vor, zwischen) can be either Dative or Accusative.
triggers.rule: Use Accusative for Movement/Direction: If you are moving from point A to point B (Question: Wohin? / Where to?).

examples.german: Ich lege das Buch auf den Tisch.
examples.translation: I am laying the book onto the table — Action/Movement.

sections.title: 5. Detailed Examples for Clarity
sections.description: Let's see the Accusative in action with different scenarios:

examples.label: Masculine Change
examples.explanation: Because the table is now the object being sold.

examples.label: No Change for Neuter/Feminine
examples.explanation: "Auto" is neuter; the article "das" does not change in the accusative.

examples.label: Using "Haben"
examples.explanation: "Job" is masculine (der Job). Since it follows haben, it must be einen.
